sentences.text: --definitely less than interviews, wasn't [INAUDIBLE].
Molly Ingster.
Mine maiden name is Bastocka, Bastocka.
B-A-S-T-O-C-K-A.
I-N-G-S-T-E-R.
1920, Vilna, Poland.
How am I feeling?
OK.
Yes.
I had a beautiful family.
I was the youngest from seven children.
My father was the [INAUDIBLE] rabbi, name, Nosen Bastocky.
And my mother was Leah Bastocky.
We had a bakery.
And we had a grocery store.
On-- on the street, [INAUDIBLE], in Vilna.
And every one of the children went to high school gymnasium.
You know?
Only my brother, the oldest one, which I met when I came to the States, was 16 years when-- my father had a big family in-- in United States.
And my mother had family in United States.
So my brother couldn't-- was-- couldn't get along with the very religious structure of our family.
Because he was the only son then.
He-- he was 23 years older than I am.
And he couldn't agree with my father's very strict religious upbringing.
And he-- so he went through United States.
My mother send him to United States.
And there he went to the university in Cleveland, Ohio.
He got a degree in electrical engineering.
And when he was 21 years old, he married.
And he became the head of director of the telephone company in Cleveland, Ohio.
And he that till he was 73 or 74 years old.
Well, mine other brother was killed by the Nazis in 1941.
They-- he was working on the streets.
They picked him up and shot him right there.
Mine oldest sister, Miriam, was taken for the first killing, actual, from the Nazis, in 1941, before the Jewish new year, Rosh Hashanah, with her husband and her three children-- or four children.
Three guests and one boy.
And the son, [?
Mayer, ?] was two years younger than me.
Because I was-- when mine sister was married, she was 17 years.
And when I was born my mother was 41.
And so somehow, mine-- my sister's son was only three years younger than me.
Mayer ?] was his name.
They all were taken to [INAUDIBLE] prison, in Vilna.
And from there, to [INAUDIBLE], to the big mass graves.
They were of the first victims from Nazi Germany.
They were killed in Vilna, from the first victims.
Yeah.
Oh, it was beautiful.
We were a very beautiful family.
My father, my mother were very, very devoted parents.
And we were all very much in love with each other.
And my father and mother did a lot of s[ What-- you know?
Charity.
Charity.
So we always had strangers at that table, eating.
And especially the-- the-- from the religious Jews, the songs used to come to Vilna.
They had to go to the army, this was a law, when they were 18 years old.
So-- and since my family was very strictly religious.
So we always had three, four young men eating at that table.
And there was-- there was a family, a mother and father, and three children, who were very poor, living across the street, in a little house.
He was-- he used to repair shoes or do shoes.
And they never had enough food.
So they always used to come around.
And my mother used to give them food.
In fact, her, the mother's name I remember.
Dobe ?] was her name.
And they always giving charity, always helping.
And there were some Polish people who lived in that building, and next building.
And they were my friends.
That was one who the father was, I think-- he was the premiere-- I don't know how to call it-- to say.
He was in the economic division in the-- in the government.
And his daughter and his son were my friends.
I was very religious.
I never could eat in their house because of kosher.
But we were very close friends.
And I remember her brother's-- his name was [INAUDIBLE].
And her name was [?
Wanda. ?] And I'm-- he was-- oh, he was the Minister of Finances in the department, in the government.
But the part of it, where in Vilna, you know?
And I forgot-- yes, she-- I forgot I have-- since I had the stroke, I forget everything.
So what can I do.
But we had a very loving family.
I had mine older sister, Miriam, as I said, the one who went first, who was taken in and killed.
Miriam, her husband was Itzak, and her son, [?
Mayer, ?] and three daughters.
Two was to [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].
The little one, when she was breastfeeding, and she was in-- in like, in the late 30's then.
I don't remember the name.
And then I had a sister, Julia, who went to Havana, Cuba, with her husband when I was eight years old.
And she's-- she lived in Cuba until Castro came to power.
Then they came to United States.
They had-- their daughter lives in Boston, who is a teacher of music.
And son-in-law is a scientist.
And they have two daughters living in California.
I was in Hebrew gymnasium.
Gymnasium [INAUDIBLE], on the [INAUDIBLE] street.
And I wanted to be a dancer.
And I wanted to be writer, and all these dreams I had.
I took dance lessons for three, four years.
And I remember, I wrote some articles in school.
And they had my picture on one of the walls.
[LAUGHS] And-- well.
And then when I was 18 years old, the war broke out, 17.
The Nazis came and I got married, very early.
In 1939, when the bombs were falling, you know, I was lying with a tiny little baby, breastfeeding in-- hiding in the-- in the cellar, you know, from the bombs.
Oh well, my husband was five years-- five or seven years older than me.
And I met him through a group of my-- my kids, you know?
And he was very much in love with me.
And I was wearing braids.
I was still a kid, you know?
And we used to go.
We had dances, you know, from [INAUDIBLE] and from school.
And that's what we used to-- you know, we had all this getting together.
He played beautiful piano.
So he used to sit by the piano.
And we used to dance.
[LAUGHS]
But it's all gone.
It's too-- it's too long ago.
You know?
And then nothing is left.
It's all disappeared.
And--
But I had a lot of Christian friends, as well as Jewish friends.
Despite the fact that we weren't religious.
But our friends were a lot of Christian.
We were very in that part where we live was more Christian than Jewish people.
And when my mother used to tell me when the First World War came, all-- Vilna was starving.
There was no bread.
There was no food, nothing.
And since the-- we had the bakery and the grocery store, and it was against the law if they would catch you in the streets, you know, these Germans, you know, I don't know, they would shoot you.
You had no right to help one another.
They want to starve everybody out.

So my mother used to tell me that at night, when it was pitch dark, they used to get-- put bags of bread and sugar and whatever they could and-- and still through the streets, nobody should see, that my mom and mine dad and my sister, and bring it to our friends, to the Christian friends, to save them.
So they saved a lot of lives during the first world war.
OK.
And in fact, that one family-- I forgot their name.
They were all, after the war, in the-- in the ministerium, very high-class people, doctors, ministers, and all this other thing.
And they were friends until the war broke out.
And then there were no more friends.
[LAUGHS] When Hitler came, there was no more friends, when the Nazis came.
Yeah.
I took ballet lessons.
And I remember the-- the teacher, Annette [?
Raiser-Kaplan. ?] It was a ballet school.
And she wanted-- she was going, before the war, to travel around the world.
And she wanted-- and to come to the United States.
And she wanted me to go.
But the old-fashioned Jewish kids, I'm going to leave my mom?
I'm going to leave my dad?
You know, it's-- I did-- if I would have left, I would have at least been-- haven't been through the hell.
Because I know that she supposedly survived in the States or Australia.
I don't know where-- where they ended up.
But they didn't go through the hell.
And I-- and I didn't want to leave my mother.
So I got it.
I danced in the theater, [INAUDIBLE], in Poland, twice solo.
And I remember the name of the dances.
Would you believe that?
You want the name?
Sure.
One is in Russian.
And one is in Hebrew.
One was [RUSSIAN].
But-- and the other one was [HEBREW].
That's a dance with a doll.
The Russian was a different solo dance.
That's the only thing I remember.
[LAUGHS]
I, myself, didn't.
No.
Because my friends were Christian.
Only in 1939-- 1938 and '39, I had a few close Christian friends.
And-- and they-- it-- it became a big propaganda to kill the Jews.
So I remember, I was walking down the streets.
And a kid, a little boy of eight or nine years old, come over to me and start saying oh, you Jew, you Jew.
You know, and using foul language.
So I run away.
I didn't know what to do.
Because if I would hit him, I would be arrested.
The last two years, when Jozef Pilsudski, the famous-- he was the general and he was also the President for a while in Poland-- was a Jewish friend form the First World War.
And the last couple of years, the influence of Nazis were very big in Poland, especially in Vilna, where I was.
And they spread it to the young people to every-- if somebody didn't have a job, it's the Jews' fault.
Anything they could.
So the anti-Semitism the last couple of years were very bad in mine town.
And the few friends what I had, the Polish friends, they stayed away form me.
They said that there's a problem.
They can't come to visit me because they will be-- they will be pointed out, that they have Jewish friends.
And it was a very bad situation, the last couple of years before the war broke out.
Because the Germany-- Nazi Germany-- there was factories in Vilna, which was belonged to the Germans.
And then when the Nazis took over, they came and-- and they had a tremendous influence, just the last couple of years, before the war.
The Jews in Vilna were very frightened.
Well, we knew we got-- I got papers and letters from-- and we-- nobody should-- we were hiding it.
Because we got it through somebody, you know, who sent us the letters and the papers.
And we had some friends there, you know?
[SIGHS]
Well, it's a beautiful Jewish community.
It's very strong.
There were very religious Jewish groups, and the people who weren't too religious.
And so you know, there-- there was plenty of synagogues.
And my father was a rabbi in one of the synagogues on [INAUDIBLE] street.
And-- but with beautiful people.
Very, very beautiful people.
And all this disappeared.
Oh.
We learned German.
Because that time, Germans were very financially-- I mean, involved in Vilna, in Poland.
And-- and I had French for a while, not long.
Because they said that German is more important.
Since if it-- if, you know, in business, like, if you want to get some kind of a job or a trade, you should know the language.
So we took German, and naturally, Hebrew.
And Yiddish, I learned at home.
And Polish, naturally.
Because that was the main language.
I-- I wanted to learn Russian.
And I learned how to speak, but not to write.
Because it was against the law.
Anybody who taught Russian in mine town before the Second World War was arrested.
Since Russia, supposedly, was the enemy of Poland, at that time.
You know?
And it's forbidden.
Russian was forbidden.
So I only learned because mine sisters.
They went to gymnasium before the First World War.
And they, at that time, they could learn-- it was-- Russia was, I think, influenced or was occupied.
Russia occupied.
I think, Vilna was.
They kept the city of Lithuania.
Russia was-- was-- was under occupation of Russia, until the Second World War.
So that's why-- no, I'm thinking the First World War.
But that time, when Poland got Vilna, Russian, if you learned Russian, if they find that someone-- if-- it was like a death sentence.
You went to prison, and that was it.
So German, Polish, speak Russian, Yiddish, Hebrew, and this is the languages.
But my sister, the one who is senile, suffers from Alzheimer, the one from Cuba, who lives in Florida.
I remember about five, six, years ago when I visited, you know, still-- she still recognize me then.
And then I ask her about-- if, you know, talking about languages, she spoke French.
She still remembers in Russian and German, and natural, Spanish and Italian.
She speaks about 10 languages.
But despite the fact that her head is not-- she started to speak to me.
And I couldn't believe it, you know, honestly.
It was beautiful.
It was great.
There was a lot of poor-- a lot of poor Jewish people, and a lot of poor people in Vilna.
But I remember we-- we used to help them.
My mother used to you know, elicit strangers eating at her table.
And many times I used to get hungry.
I was, I don't know, about 10, 12 years.
And I used to say to her, you know, over the weekends and holidays, I said, ma, why can't we have just our family?
Because I had my grandfather and mine uncles.
And my cousins used to come.
We were 20, 30 people, you know?
At a big table-- table that was as long as this room.
[LAUGHS] And he said to me, shh.
My child, don't ever complain and say a thing.
Be grateful, she said, that you can give and help.
But the last several years, at times, were very bad because of anti-Semitism and boycotting the Jewish stores, and the Jewish business.
So we-- my mother had-- we struggled very hard to survive.
You know?
This was like, 1935, something, from-- till-- because when that Jozef Pilsudski was the President.
And he-- he was a very good man.
And I think his wife was Jewish or something.
I don't know.
He treated the Jews equally.
But when he died, the anti-Semitism in full flare.
And Germany, naturally, was under the Nazis already.
And with tremendous influence.
And going [INAUDIBLE] my parents struggled very hard to survive.
It was very hard.
For us?
It started in 1939, when the bombs were falling over our head.
They were bombing.
And-- and then Hitler made a pact with Stalin.
Stalin kept-- Vilna was a part of Lithuania because Vilna-- the-- the Russians took.
And the other part, Hitler took.
So Warsaw was under Hitler.
Yeah.
Well, we had no-- no deals with them.
I have-- I can't-- there-- That was such a short time that I really have no memories.
They came in 1940.
Some times, I don't know which month.
And six months later, I think, the Nazis came in.
It was only six months, they were there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know when the Nazis came in.
It was June 21, 1941.
Yeah.
Well, we knew.
We used to get papers, you know?
I told you, not officially because it was through friends, to-- to some that they're killing the Jews.
And they're torturing, in the ghetto.
And they have no food in there.
And we didn't want to believe it.
It was unbelievable.
Because my mother had very close friends from before the war, first war, Germans.
And they used to do business with Germany.
My father used to deliver trucks, wagons of flour.
You know?
For Germany.
And this is-- I'm talking for the First World War, you see?
I wasn't there.
So you know, the first war.
And then-- and my mother had German friends.
And-- and we were close.
And she couldn't believe, when she read this, and heard about the Jews were tortured, you know, and killed, and things like that happened in-- in Warsaw and the other towns.
She says, how is it possible?
They're-- they're such cultural people, such nice people.
I have so many good friends.
I can believe that the Germans would do it.
She didn't want to believe it.
Until it hit her, when they came to us.
The Soviets came Vilna.
They-- they had a pact with Hitler.
There was no bombing and no nothing.
It was just-- they came in very, very-- without fight, without anything.
Can't remember.
Because they were only a few months.
So I don't think-- this was sometimes in-- in-- in 1940, I don't know when, in 1941, already, Hitler-- Nazis came.
So--
Yeah.
It-- no, I think the Poles kept quiet.
I think so.
I'm not sure.
Because they-- they were afraid for the-- for the Soviets too, for the Russians.
So they kept quiet, I think.
I can't remember.
I thought-- but that's what it was, I think so.
I had a little baby, you know?
We were-- they were going-- they were driving in with the tanks, with the big tanks.
They came, you know, and heil Hitler.
And the Poles were so happy to see them.
Sorry I have to say that.
But the Poles were so happy.
They were waving to them.
And-- and heil Hitler.
Could you believe that?
Because the-- the Russians, they were afraid for the Russians.
Because they were such anti-Semites, you know?
But when the-- when the Germans came in, they became their friends.
They didn't realize that the Germans will kill them too.
They didn't want to face the facts.
You mentioned you were with your baby when the-- the bombs were falling?
I was running in the cellars, you know, hiding from the bombing.
You know?
It's funny, like, three, four buildings were bombed out.
But the building where I lived wasn't bombed out.
Could you believe that?
But we had to run all the way-- I was on the fourth floor.
We had to go-- fourth or the fifth, I think-- all the way in the cellars, hiding.
How did things begin to change for the Jews?
Well, right away it was-- you couldn't go out certain hours of the day.
It became hell, OK?
They start to grab the Jews, and take them to kill them, to-- in mass graves.
They used to shot them.
My brother was killed on the street, walking.
Shot.
And so was my brother-in-law, one of my sisters' husbands.
And [INAUDIBLE] husband was killed.
And she was-- this-- she was told that he was taken to the [INAUDIBLE], where the-- the police station.
So she was running.
I was running with her to the police station.
If she can't see him, she was crying.
Because she was pregnant.
She had a baby in ghetto.
And she can't see her husband.
She can't do this.
So they threw her out.
So what I want to say, in the beginning, the Poles cooperated very much with the Nazis.
And wait and see, when somebody of the Poles read this story.
And they will say no, this never happened.
She's lying.
But I know.
How did things changed for you now, with the German invasion?
I mean, I lost everything.
I lost my husband.
I lost my brother.
I lost my sisters.
Half of my family was already killed, for the first few months.
And then they pushed us in the ghetto.
And i was in the ghetto with my baby and my mother.
Then my mother was taken, went to the other ghetto, the smaller ghetto.
But then-- but then-- mine's brother-in-law, the other one, from my sister, Rivka, they-- there was a factory for fur.
They gathered all the furs from the Jewish woman.
And they remade them.
And they send it to their wives in Germany.
So they needed slave work.
So they grabbed all the Jews up.
Like, they made, like, a little ghetto.
And this were on the outskirts of town.
And that's where my sister, Rivka, was with her children, two boys and her husband.
And my sister, Chasia, was for-- with-- she had a baby in ghetto.
And she-- and then my mother went with them.
Because I was-- they moved me from one block in the ghetto to the other.
And they-- and they probably was planning, at that time, to wipe out the ghetto completely.
So my mother went with them, there.
And--
OK.
So hard for me to remember this.
